snippet.title: Gunfire damages vehicles and home in Grand Island
snippet.description:
“We got a phone call from the residence at that address on 17th street," Elliot mentioned. "They heard gunshots.”

Elliott then said the victims walked outside and can’t be 100% sure if it was related, but noticed their windshields shattered and stomped on.

“Footprints or shoeprints were seen on the dust on the vehicles and then they noticed there were gunshots or bullet holes in the vehicles, as well,” Elliott noted.

According to Elliott, the attack appeared to be targeted.

“We're hoping that our victims cooperate and help us to identify who our suspects are and then bring those suspects to justice,” Elliott announced.

In recent years, GIPD has committed itself to reducing crime in the city. In fact, in 2024, the crime rate dropped by just over 14% with goals of continuing to decrease that number by 15-20% in 2025 and Chief Elliott told me they were hoping for 10-15% reduction at this point in the year.

Read more: https://nebraska.tv/news/local/gunfire-damages-vehicles-and-home-in-grand-island#

snippet.title: Huskers Wrap Up Final Practice Before Opener in Kansas City vs.Cincinnati
snippet.description:
Nebraska wrapped up its final preseason football practice in Lincoln on Tuesday before turning its focus to Thursday night’s opener against Cincinnati in Kansas City.

Head coach Matt Rhule said he is eager to man the sidelines under the bright lights.

“The team’s been great. I think they want to play — it’s very evident, a very mature team,” Rhule said.

As he enters his third season in Lincoln, Rhule emphasized that he wants this year to feel different, starting with himself.

“I very much made a commitment this year to having fun in everything that I do because it’s the type of team we have,” Rhule said.

Rhule added that the Huskers are more prepared for the opener than in past years.

“We’re significantly further ahead in terms of how we handle a game excited to see them actually play it out,” he said.

Part of the preseason preparation included film sessions reviewing dozens of plays from last year. Rhule said the team approached it with “humility and accountability,” noting that too many “bad football plays” stood out.

Ten Blackshirts were awarded this week, a tradition dating back to 1964, with the hope of turning preparation into better play on the field.

“Just challenging the guys — we want to go play this game one snap at a time, with intentional focus, a neutral mindset and attack each play,” Rhule said.

The Huskers will travel to Kansas City as a team on Wednesday, hold a walkthrough, meet together, and even share a movie night watching “Superman” before Thursday’s 8 p.m. kickoff.

snippet.description:
Defensive lineman Keona Davis is locked in. In this fall camp media session, Davis breaks down the progress he’s made with technique, praises the energy of Coach Bradden and sets big personal goals for the season. This  includesdouble-digit sacks and tackles.

snippet.title: Nebraska Refocused After Bye Week, Rested and Ready to Face Michigan State
snippet.description:
Coming out of a bye week, Nebraska coach Matt Rhule said his team is rested, refocused, and ready to respond after a three-point loss to Michigan.

Rhule pointed to the work ethic during the off week as a sign of progress, noting players like tight end Luke Lenemeyer, who was out on the practice field doing drills on his own.

“We came out of the bye week feeling really good about a lot of guys in terms of where they’re at,” Rhule said. “I thought the team got a lot better last week.”

Nebraska (3-1, 0-1 Big Ten) will host Michigan State (3-1, 1-0) on Saturday. The Spartans are averaging more than 34 points per game behind quarterback Aidan Chiles, who has completed over 70% of his passes this season while also posing a threat as a runner.

“They’re a pro-style offense, and he does all the pro-style things,” Rhule said. “He’s taking what’s there, protecting the football. He’s one of the better quarterbacks in the Big Ten.”

Statistically, Nebraska brings one of the nation’s most explosive passing attacks, averaging 352 yards per game — second best in the FBS. But Rhule acknowledged that the Huskers’ running game and rush defense still need improvement. Nebraska is allowing 173 rushing yards per contest, which ranks outside the top 100 nationally.

On the injury front, Rhule said cornerback Malcolm Hartzog is doubtful to play. Hartzog had the game-clinching interception against Cincinnati earlier this month.

Kickoff against Michigan State is set for 3 p.m. Saturday at Memorial Stadium.

#Huskers #cornhuskers #huskerfootball #dylanraiola #lukelindenmeyer #emmettjohnson #nebraskafootball #nebraskafb #ncaafootball

snippet.title: Top Draft Pick Caglione Prepares for Big League Outfield Role
snippet.description:
The Kansas City Royals are chasing a playoff berth and could soon get a boost from their top draft pick a year ago, Jack Caglione. But before the power-hitting prospect can join the big-league lineup, he has to master a new position — the outfield.

Caglione, known as “Jack Cags,” has been working on the transition this summer with the Royals' Triple-A team, the Omaha Storm Chasers. A natural infielder and pitcher, he has spent the last several weeks learning the intricacies of the outfield with the help of Royals coach Rusty Kuntz, a former big leaguer who played in the 1980s.

“I’m pretty comfortable out there,” Caglione said. “There are still some plays I’m not used to seeing. It’s a lot different than first, but that’s the learning process. Thankfully, Rusty’s out here helping me.”

The Royals value Caglione’s bat — he has already shown home run power in Triple-A — but need him to become dependable defensively before he makes the jump to Kansas City. Kuntz said the focus has been on fundamentals like taking proper routes and getting under control to make plays.

“I’ve got to remember that he was not an outfielder,” Kuntz said. “He was an infielder and a pitcher, and now some of the things I just throw at him, I have to step back and go, ‘Wait a minute, we’re still learning this process.’”

Caglione, meanwhile, is keeping a close eye on the standings.

“I’d love to be playing in October,” he said.

Kuntz believes the young slugger has the mentality to get there.

“He’s an absolute dream because he’s a baseball player first,” Kuntz said. “He just wants to come out and compete, and he’ll work his butt off. That’s a coach’s dream.”

The Royals expect Caglione to rejoin the team in September as they push toward the postseason.

snippet.title: Nebraska Ready for Michigan State After Two-Week Break
snippet.description:
The Cornhuskers have had a fortnight to stew over their loss to Michigan, but head coach Matt Rhule said his team is ready to put that behind them as they prepare to face Michigan State on Saturday.

“The week’s been great,” Rhule said Friday during his final press conference of the week at Memorial Stadium. “They practiced hard last week. They practiced hard this week, so they’ll be ready on Saturday.”

The weather could play a role in the matchup. With high winds and temperatures expected in the mid-80s, Rhule said Nebraska has been preparing for all conditions. “It’ll be a factor in everything that you do,” he said. “It might mean you make some different decisions than you normally would. It’s part of being in the region of the country that we’re in.”

Nebraska has not defeated Michigan State since 2018, but Rhule is confident in his team’s preparation. “I’m expecting our players to go play really, really well. I think they’ve improved. We’re just gonna play it out and see what happens,” he said.

Kickoff against Michigan State is set for 3 p.m. Saturday at Memorial Stadium.

snippet.title: Rhule Previews Huskers vs. Michigan in Big Ten Opener
snippet.description:
Nebraska head coach Matt Rhule previewed the Huskers’ first Big Ten game of the season, a home matchup Saturday against No. 21 Michigan.

The Wolverines (2-1) enter the contest without head coach Sherrone Moore, who is serving the second game of a three-game suspension for NCAA violations. Interim coach Biff Poggi, who previously coached at Charlotte, will lead Michigan after the team’s 63-3 win over Central Michigan last weekend.

The quarterback battle is expected to headline the game. Michigan will start true freshman Bryce Underwood in his first Big Ten appearance, while Nebraska (3-0) turns to sophomore second-year starter Dylan Raiola. Raiola has thrown for 829 yards, eight touchdowns, and no interceptions through three games, leading an offense that ranks fifth nationally in passing at 366 yards per game.

“If we have great protection — and we’ve had great protection — then the quarterback’s going to be unbelievably efficient,” Rhule said.

Nebraska’s defense has been equally impressive, allowing just 66 passing yards per game to lead the nation. Michigan ranks 81st in passing offense at 212 yards per game and 43rd in passing defense at 182.

Rhule emphasized balance and execution as keys to Nebraska’s success. The Huskers have not had a three-and-out in the past two games but continue to focus on red-zone efficiency.

“We’ve always had a period for it, but we’ll add some emphasis this week and try to get better,” Rhule said.

On the injury front, Rhule confirmed defensive backs Malcolm Hartzog and Javin Wright will return after missing last week’s game as a precaution.

Rhule, who attended Terence “Bud” Crawford’s recent fight in Las Vegas, said he wants his team to seize its own opportunity on the national stage against a ranked opponent.

Kickoff between Nebraska and Michigan is set for Saturday in Lincoln at 2:30 PM CST.

#Huskers #cornhuskers #huskerfootball #dylanraiola #nebraskafootball #nebraskafb #ncaafootball #mattrhule

snippet.title: Ricketts and Osborn trade barbs in NE Senate race
snippet.description:
KEARNEY, Neb. — Senator Pete Ricketts made a stop in Kearney as part of his statewide reelection campaign, where he took aim at his opponent, Dan Osborn. Ricketts accused Osborn of being a Democrat who would obstruct former President Trump's agenda.

"You can bet that's where he's going to put his allegiance if he were to get elected. That's why it's so important that we beat fake Dan Osborn and send a real conservative to Washington," said Ricketts.

Osborn, who previously ran against Deb Fischer in 2024 and made national headlines when he garnered 46.7% of the vote, is also conducting a series of town halls. He previously emphasized his opposition to the "One Big Beautiful Bill" and his connection to the working class, contrasting his background with Ricketts'.

"Ricketts is certainly a bigger contrast from me. This story writes itself about his background and my background," said Osborn.

Ricketts' event featured remarks from Representative Adrian Smith and local officials, including Kearney Mayor Jonathan Nikkila, State Senator Stan Clouse and State Board of Education Representative Sherry Jones. In contrast, Osborn's previous town hall in Hastings was more informal and included a question-and-answer session.

snippet.title: Malcom Hartzog’s Late Interception Seals Nebraska Win Over Cincinnati in Arrowhead Opener
snippet.description:
Nebraska defensive back Malcolm Hartzog Jr. intercepted a pass in the end zone with 34 seconds remaining, sealing a season-opening victory for the Huskers at Arrowhead Stadium on Saturday night.

The play capped a night where first-year defensive coordinator John Butler’s emphasis on situational football paid off. “Yeah, this is going to build us some confidence for sure,” Hartzog said. “A lot of people say we can’t win a close game, so winning that close game right there just makes us know we can do it.”

While the defense made the final stop, the Huskers will look to shore up their rushing defense before next week’s matchup against Akron. Nebraska allowed 202 rushing yards on 30 carries.

On the offensive side, new starting running back Emmett Johnson delivered in his debut. Johnson carried the ball 25 times for more than 108 yards and added 10 receptions for 27 yards. “Feels great, man, but I can only give credit to my O-line,” Johnson said. “I’m willing to do whatever it takes for this team to win.”

 Sophomore Quarterback Dylan Raiola, entering his second season as the starter, threw for 243 yards and two touchdowns, including a fourth-and-2 conversion to transfer wide receiver Dane Key. Raiola said the win was encouraging but noted room for improvement. “There’s some third downs, some fourth downs that we wish we could have had back, but that’s game one and the growing pains,” Raiola said. “I’m just glad we left here with a W.”

Raiola had ice on his hand postgame but did not comment on any injury. Head coach Matt Rhule also did not mention it.

Nebraska fans dominated the Arrowhead crowd of more than 70,000. Rhule estimated 65,000 supporters made the trip south to Kansas City, creating an atmosphere reminiscent of Memorial Stadium.

The Huskers (1-0) return to Lincoln next Saturday for their home opener at Memorial Stadium against Akron. Kickoff is set for 6:30 p.m.

snippet.title: Nebraska Volleyball: Dani Busboom Kelly Breaks Down Passing, Offense, and Illinois/Minnesota Prep
snippet.description:
Nebraska head coach Dani Busboom Kelly meets with the media to discuss the Huskers’ upcoming matches and where the team is trending. She says she isn’t concerned about passing, noting the team is putting up strong numbers and that the criticism is mostly nitpicking. She highlights Bergen’s strong play, the historic offensive numbers the team is producing, and the mindset of taking things point by point instead of overanalyzing individual moments.

Dani talks about the consistency of hitting .300 as a team and how impressive it is to do it night after night with a balanced offense. She credits Bergen for blocking well, sticking to game plans, and making adjustments even when those plans change from match to match. She also expands on her coaching philosophy and how the team continues to grow with clean play and high-level execution.

Looking ahead, Dani says Illinois is off to a great start and points out their setter, who she played against in her Louisville days when the setter was at Florida State. She says Illinois’ outside is playing at a high level and that it’s always fun to coach against former assistants. She also previews Minnesota, a team dealing with injuries but still staying in the top 25 with one of the best outsides in the country. She expects a challenge on the road.

Dani closes by talking about what it means for high school teams to get to play at the Devaney Center and how big of a deal that experience is.

snippet.title: Huskers Volleyball Prep for Alumni Match
snippet.description:
The Nebraska volleyball team is gearing up for its annual alumni match Saturday at the Bob Devaney Sports Center, now home to the newly named John Cook Arena.

Head coach Dani Busboom Kelly said she’s eager for her team to face new opponents while also seeing familiar faces from the program’s past.

“You’d like to think we’re going to be quite a bit more organized, so we have an edge, but you can’t count out Olympians,” Busboom Kelly said. “Some of these players haven’t played in the last month, but they just finished pro seasons, and we’re playing at a really high level. I’m expecting them to be pretty good on the other side.”

Among the alumni returning are stars such as Jordan Larson, Lauren Stiverins, Kenzie Maloney, Allie Badenhorst, and Lindsey Krause. For Busboom Kelly, the focus is on sharpening fundamentals and testing the depth of her roster.

“Our practices are really tough,” she said. “That’s just going to make us stronger in the end. That’s why I’m very excited to play against somebody else.”

With the alumni match only six days before Nebraska’s first game in the AVCA Kickoff, the contest serves as a true test of where the team stands.

Outside hitter Harper Murray said fall practice has been intense. “Two-a-days are really hard, but we get through it together,” she said. “I’m looking forward to facing some familiar faces and meeting some of the alumni. It’s gonna be fun playing against them.”

Assistant coach and alum Kelly Hunter Natter will also step onto the court for the match. “It’s really exciting,” Natter said. “It’s a good chance to see a lot of my former teammates and alums that you don’t get to see all that often.”

Busboom Kelly said if the alumni match proves successful, the program hopes to make it an annual tradition, similar to other top volleyball programs.

The match takes place Saturday, August 16th at 6 PM at the Bob Devaney Sports Center.

snippet.title: Nebraska Volleyball Ready to Make History Playing at PBA
snippet.description:
Nebraska volleyball is preparing to make its debut at Pinnacle Bank Arena, a stage the program has never played on before. While the Huskers have hosted games at Memorial Stadium and the Devaney Sports Center, Friday’s matchup against Pitt will mark the first time the team takes the court in the 16,000-seat arena.

“24 hours from now, 16,000 fans will pack it out, seeing Big Red and PBA for the first time,” Nebraska head coach Dani Busboom-Kelly said. “I feel like we’ve done everything as a program, and this is one thing I get to do as a head coach, so that’s pretty cool.”

Team captains say the experience feels monumental.

“It feels like we’re in December already, like a Final Four environment,” junior setter Bergren Reilly said. “We know the fans are going to bring it, which will make it even better.”

Being named captain is an honor, the players said, but not the team’s ultimate goal.

“It’s a big honor to be a captain at Nebraska, but everybody knows that’s not the main goal of the season,” said Reilly. 

Senior middle blocker Rebekah Allick said, “It’s something I’ll get to look back on, but right now it doesn’t change much. We already have so many leaders.”

The team has practiced at the Devaney Sports Center and says the transition to PBA will be smooth.

“It’ll be really cool to have a big home-court advantage,” Reilly said. “We get to be in a bigger place than Devaney, and we’ve watched a lot of other events here. It’s exciting to be one of them.”

The Huskers will face Pitt at 6 PM CST on Friday in the first match at Pinnacle Bank Arena.

snippet.title: Huskers Football Big Win vs. Cinci | Huskers Volleyball Reverse Sweep vs. KY | NTV Sports Pod 16
snippet.description:
It was a huge weekend for the Huskers, and we break it all down on this episode.

Nebraska football opened the season with a 20–17 win over Cincinnati at Arrowhead Stadium. Sophomore quarterback Dylan Raiola threw for 243 yards and two touchdowns, running back Emmett Johnson added 108 yards on the ground, and Malcolm Hartzog Jr. sealed the game with an interception in the end zone with 34 seconds left. The victory marked Nebraska’s first season-opening win over a Power Five opponent since 2003.

On the volleyball court, No. 1 Nebraska stormed back to reverse sweep No. 7 Kentucky in Nashville. After dropping the first two sets, the Huskers responded with a 25–19, 25–23, 15–8 finish to claim the match. Harper Murray led the way with a career-high 23 kills, hitting .340 with 15 digs and 5 blocks. Key lineup changes by head coach Dani Busboom Kelly after set 2 included Olivia Mauch stepping in at libero and Virginia Adriano at opposite, which helped spark the comeback. Nebraska outhit Kentucky .221 to .168 and won the dig battle 73–60.

Both teams showed grit and resilience, setting the tone for the season ahead.

snippet.title: Kearney restaurant owners respond to backlash over Kirk post.
snippet.description:
KEARNEY, Neb. — A local Kearney business owner has spoken out to clarify remarks she made on social media following the shooting of Charlie Kirk, a few weeks ago. Which sparked significant backlash and misunderstanding.

Cindy Houlden, silent partner at The Lodge Restaurant, addressed the controversy surrounding a Facebook post that was quickly shared and taken out of context. She explained that her original post was online for less than a minute and that only part of it was circulated widely.

“Our grandmother used to say there’s three sides to every story: his side, her side, and the truth. Well, there’s three sides to this story, too," said Houlden.

To clarify what was actually said online, Houlden emphasized that she did not justify the shooting in any way. Instead, her message focused on the broader consequences of division and hatred in the country. “What I meant by karma is the karma we all have to live with with our actions as we go forward,” she said. She expressed concern that ongoing societal divisions could lead to further tragedies.

“My post said that what happened to Charlie Kirk is not justified in any way shape or form," added Houlden. "No matter how divisive some people may have found his words, but with that said what I meant by karma is the karma we all have to live with, with our actions as we go forward.”

Despite her intentions to promote reflection and unity, Houlden and the business faced immediate backlash. Threatening comments flooded her social media, prompting Facebook to warn her about potential threats. Sales at The Lodge have reportedly dropped significantly.

“We’ve seen a major impact on the sales and what’s happened to the business,” noted Robert James, The Lodge Restaurant Co-Owner and Kitchen Manager.

This crushes James. “It’s breaking my heart to see it die,” he said.

Hatred didn’t stop with simply attacking the owners and their business. Houlden mentioned threats were made against her employees including coming after high schoolers. “When this initially happened, we lost 80% of our workforce,” said James.

The threats extended beyond the owners to employees, including young workers and local students. In response, Houlden contacted Kearney Public Schools and the University of Nebraska at Kearney to alert them to threats circulating online.

Houlden called for open dialogue and community unity, urging people to come together over shared meals and conversations. “When you have children, is this the world you want them to grow up in?” she asked.

She said she hopes to stop what she called destructive behavior. “And I feel it towards both sides, but I’ve never felt the hate from the left that I feel from the right. I’ll be honest,” noted Houlden.

She also clarified her role in the business, stating that James primarily owns and operates The Lodge while she manages the books and finances. Both owners have expressed disbelief at how the situation escalated.

The incident highlights growing tensions in communities across the country, underscoring the challenges faced by individuals trying to express nuanced views in a highly polarized environment.

snippet.title: Nebraska Football Head Coach Matt Rhule Signs Contract Extension
snippet.description:
Stability is the theme in Lincoln as Nebraska head coach Matt Rhule has signed a contract extension that keeps him with the Cornhuskers through 2032.

Rhule, who originally signed a deal through 2030, said earlier this year that he wanted to stay in Lincoln — and made good on that statement by putting pen to paper.

“I’m very, very grateful to Troy Dannen, to UNL President Gold, to the board for their confidence in me and our staff,” Rhule said. “Very grateful to my wife and kids who allowed me to do this job and moved here. I’m very grateful to the fan base, the city of Lincoln, and the state of Nebraska. This has become our home.”

Rhule added that the new deal is about stability and long-term success, not just for himself but for the program.

“Hopefully it sends a message to recruits and players that this is the place to be — and we want to be here,” Rhule said. “I love this team and I love where we’re headed.”

Nebraska Athletic Director Troy Dannen said conversations about the extension began over the summer and intensified around Week 3 of the season.

“I believe in the program. I believe in Matt,” Dannen said. “We needed to get the buyout reset if we possibly could. That’s really why I initiated the discussions when I did.”

The new contract does not include a base salary increase but does reset the buyout and add performance-based incentives. Rhule will continue to earn $7.5 million this year, increasing to $12.5 million by 2030. Each time Nebraska reaches the College Football Playoff Round of 16, his base pay rises by another $1 million.

Since arriving in Lincoln, Rhule has guided the Huskers to their first winning season in eight years and their first bowl victory since 2015. This fall, Nebraska is 6–2, marking the program’s best eight-game start in nine seasons.

“We could have gone somewhere else probably — but we want to be here,” Rhule said. “Not like, ‘Hey, we’ve got to be here.’ We want to be here.”

For Nebraska fans and recruits alike, the message is clear: Rhule, Dannen, and the administration are united — and they believe the best years for Cornhusker football are still ahead.

snippet.title: Freund Family Foods keep NE State Fairgoers hydrated
Reactions and Thoughts from NTV's Sports Team After Nebraska Loses to Michigan
snippet.description:
In a tightly contested Big Ten opener, No. 21 Michigan edged Nebraska 30–27 on Saturday at Memorial Stadium in Lincoln. The Wolverines improved to 3–1 overall and 1–0 in conference play, while the Cornhuskers fell to 3–1 and 0–1 in the Big Ten. 

Michigan's offense was propelled by a dominant ground game, amassing 286 rushing yards. Running back Justice Haynes led the charge with 149 yards on 17 carries, including a 75-yard touchdown run. Freshman quarterback Bryce Underwood added 105 passing yards and a 37-yard touchdown run. Jordan Marshall contributed with a 54-yard touchdown run, marking his first significant play of the season. 
Maize n Brew

Defensively, the Wolverines were relentless, recording seven sacks and 10 tackles for loss. Linebacker Cole Sullivan had a standout performance with an interception, a sack, and six tackles. True freshman safety Jayden Sanders stepped up in the absence of Zeke Berry, recording eight tackles and solid coverage on crucial third downs. 
Maize n Brew

Nebraska's quarterback Dylan Raiola threw for 308 yards and three touchdowns, connecting with Jacory Barney Jr. for two scores. Running back Emmett Johnson added 67 rushing yards, but the Huskers' offense struggled to establish a consistent ground game, totaling just 43 rushing yards. 
Wikipedia

The game featured dramatic momentum swings. After trailing 10–0, Nebraska rallied to tie the game at 17–17 by halftime, highlighted by a 52-yard Hail Mary touchdown pass from Raiola to Barney Jr. However, Michigan's second-half scoring, including a 56-yard field goal and a 54-yard touchdown run, proved decisive. 

Michigan's victory extended its winning streak against Nebraska to five games and improved its all-time series lead to 9–4–1. The Wolverines also secured their sixth consecutive win in a Big Ten opener. 
University of Michigan Athletics

Nebraska will look to rebound in its next conference matchup, aiming to build on the resilience shown in a hard-fought game against a ranked opponent.

snippet.title: Dani Busboom Kelly's Era at Nebraska Starts with Big Wins with Laney Choboy Shining at Libero
snippet.description:
LINCOLN, Neb. — The Dani Busboom Kelly era of Nebraska volleyball is off to a dominant start. The Huskers opened the season with back-to-back wins over top-six opponents (3. Pittsburgh and 6. Stanford), and another top-10 challenge looms with No. 7 Kentucky next.

The early success has come with an emphasis on defense and chemistry. Players say the support within the team has been just as important as the competition.

“Yes, we push each other every day — every practice is a battle,” freshman outsider hitter Teraya Sigler said. “But we are also so happy for each other and willing to support each other in whatever that looks like. Everyone is all in with wanting to be the best teammates they can.”

That buy-in has been especially evident at libero, a position that has produced some of Nebraska’s most iconic names, including Busboom Kelly herself. This fall, sophomore Laney Choboy has stepped into the role vacated by All-American Lexi Rodriguez.

Choboy said her growth has been as much mental as physical. “It wasn’t really my skill that improved, it was my mentality,” she said. “What I was going to let affect me, what I was going to let into my body and my heart. The mental side made the biggest difference.”

She acknowledged the challenge of following Rodriguez, but said she has embraced it. “I learned from Lexi and will continue to use her as a resource, but I’m also my own player,” Choboy said. “I can do this. While I’m stepping into the role she left behind, I’m coming into it on my own.”

Carrying the libero torch is not lost on Choboy, who called it a dream come true. “Nebraska has a legacy of big-time liberos,” she said. “Justine and Lexi going on to the Olympics — it’s insane to think I’m in this position now. I’m just grateful to be here and hope to carry on that legacy.”

Busboom Kelly said her team is embracing the challenges ahead, including Kentucky.

“I’m excited to play Kentucky,” she said. “This weekend was a challenge, and Kentucky has the talent to be a team in December that makes it all the way, in my opinion. It’ll be a heck of a challenge for us.”

With Busboom Kelly at the helm, Nebraska’s start to 2025 already looks like the foundation for another historic run.

snippet.title: Nebraska Football Ready for Road Test at Maryland
snippet.description:
Nebraska football coach Matt Rhule says his team is ready for a big-time matchup against the 4-1 Maryland Terrapins this weekend. The Huskers, also 4-1, will travel to face Maryland.

“They respect the heck out of the team that we’re facing,” Rhule said during his weekly press conference on Thursday. “They know the battle that it’s going to be. That’s the message, and our guys have gotten that loud and clear.”

Rule confirmed that defensive back Malcolm Hartzog Jr. is doubtful and likely will not travel with the team. With a Northeaster expected to impact the East Coast, Nebraska practiced with wet footballs all week to prepare for potential rainy conditions.

Nebraska’s defense has been a key strength this season. Against Michigan State last week, the Huskers recorded five sacks and 14 quarterback hurries, consistently pressuring the pocket. Maryland’s true freshman quarterback, Malik Washington, has passed for 1,257 yards with nine touchdowns and two interceptions. Rhule described Washington as a dual-threat player, but Nebraska boasts the nation’s top-ranked pass defense, allowing just 90 yards per game.

“With the possibility of rain, the passing game may not see much action,” Rhule said. “That means our front seven have to step up and create the energy."

The Huskers’ offense also faces pressure. Quarterback Dylan Raiola has been sacked 12 times over the past two games — seven against Michigan and five against Michigan State. 

Rhule emphasized that not all of the responsibility lies with the offensive line, but they know improvement is needed. Protecting Raiola will be crucial for Nebraska’s offense, which ranks eighth nationally at 320 yards per game.

Kick off is Saturday at 2:30 p.m. Central.

snippet.title: Nebraska cannabis board faces delays after resignations and legal charges
snippet.description:
The Nebraska Medical Cannabis Commission Board convened Tuesday despite the recent resignations of members Bruce Bailey and Kim Lowe, who stepped down at the request of Gov. Jim Pillen. The resignations followed the indictment of Hobert Rupe, executive director of the Liquor Control Commission, on seven charges. Prosecutors allege Rupe provided favors to a Lincoln strip club owner in exchange for cash, dances, and sexual favors. The reasons behind Bailey and Lowe's resignations remain unclear, but Gov. Pillen expressed gratitude for their service, stating, "I appreciate the willingness of Bruce and Kim to serve, and I thank them for the time they have been on the commission."

By law, members of the Liquor Control Commission also serve on the Medical Cannabis Commission. Gov. Pillen is currently accepting applications to fill the vacancies and will appoint two new members. The board faced an October 1 deadline to approve the state's four cannabis cultivators and release permanent regulations. Monica Oldenburg confirmed that one task has been completed: "The emergency regulations approved at the September 8 meeting have been approved and signed by the governor and are in full force and in effect until 11:59 p.m. on December 7, at which time they will expire unless extended for a second 90-day period."

Despite receiving 39 cultivator applications, the board could not select the four cultivators. An evaluation team, which included Bailey and Lowe, was tasked with reviewing the applications. The commission intended for the team to provide licensure recommendations at Tuesday's meeting. Lorelle Mueting emphasized the board's commitment to resolving the issue, saying, "We just ask, I guess, for your understanding that this is a situation that none of us created and this is where we’re at right here. We hope you understand that we’re working through this the best we can to get the licenses issued in a timely manner."

Read more: https://nebraska.tv/news/local/nebraska-cannabis-board-faces-delays-after-resignations-and-legal-charges#

snippet.title: Huskers Show Grit in 28–21 Win over Northwestern, Bounce Back from Minnesota Loss
snippet.description:
Nebraska head coach Matt Rhule pushed back on the idea that the Huskers lost focus in preparation for last week’s loss at Minnesota, saying his team “did not take our feet off the gas pedal” as they now turn their attention to Northwestern.

The response came after tight end Luke Lindemeier said Tuesday that Nebraska lost focus in the week leading up to the 24–6 setback in Minneapolis. Rhule disagreed, emphasizing that both last week and this week have featured strong practices.

“I thought we had a great week of work,” Rhule said. “Our guys haven’t taken their foot off the gas.”

Nebraska will need that urgency against a Northwestern offense built on a physical ground game. The Wildcats are averaging 190 rushing yards per game, sixth best in the Big Ten. Rhule praised Northwestern’s offensive front and explosive running backs.

“They’re unbelievably well-coached up front,” Rhule said. “Their backs have great bursts and they’re as athletic as we’ll see. They have home-run ability.”

The matchup puts pressure on a Nebraska defense ranked 15th in the Big Ten in rushing defense, allowing 157 yards per game. However, if the Huskers can keep the Wildcats behind the sticks, the advantage flips. Nebraska owns the nation’s top-ranked pass defense, while Northwestern enters with the No. 112 passing offense.

“You can’t let 1st-and-10 become 2nd-and-5,” Rhule said. “It’s going to come down to our ability to get off blocks and make tackles.”

Nebraska received mixed injury news entering the weekend. Starting right guard Rocco Spindler, who left the Minnesota game with a finger injury, is expected to play. Rhule called the decision a matter of pain tolerance and said Spindler practiced all week. “He’s a baller,” Rhule said.

On the other side of the ledger, defensive back Malcolm Hartzog will miss the remainder of the season. He will redshirt after undergoing surgery for a core-muscle injury.

Nebraska (5–2, 2–2 Big Ten) and Northwestern (5–2, 3–1) kick off at 11 a.m. Saturday at Memorial Stadium.

snippet.title: Matt Rhule Press Conference for Houston Christian | Nebraska Football Presser
snippet.description:
LINCOLN — Nebraska head coach Matt Rhule opened his end-of-week press conference with reflection and perspective as the Huskers turn their attention to Houston Christian.

Rhule said the team visited the 9/11 Memorial before their bowl game in New York last season, an experience he believes gave players valuable perspective. “It’s important to remind them what it means, to encourage our guys to tell people you love them,” Rhule said. He also congratulated Nebraska’s newest Hall of Fame inductees, highlighting Brett Maher’s rare achievement of earning All-Big Ten honors as both a punter and kicker.

Rhule shared his own memories of Sept. 11, 2001, when he was a coach at UCLA. “I remember the helpless feeling of being in Los Angeles, watching it in the facilities,” he said. With a family member who died in a plane crash, Rhule said the tragedy hit close to home. He emphasized the importance of bringing those stories to life for players who weren’t yet born.

Looking ahead, Rhule stressed this week is about improvement. “If we don’t play better than we played last week, then we didn’t play better,” he said. The coach added that he was pleased with practice and with a recent video the program released, saying it set the standard he expects.

Rhule also weighed in on the NCAA transfer portal, questioning why it doesn’t align with the financial year. “Why don’t we just do what the NFL does? They’re doing so well,” he said.

Quarterback Dylan Raiola continues to be a focal point, with Saturday marking his 16th career game. “He’s still so young in his journey, but he’s doing it at such an advanced level,” Rhule said. “He’s at level four now with his understanding. We’ve elevated what we do with him, and he’s taken it to a whole other level.”

Houston Christian’s defense presents challenges, Rhule added, noting they’ve allowed only one third-down conversion this season while constantly showing different looks. “We have to do a better job getting to the quarterback,” he said.

Rhule also praised left tackle Gunnar Gottula, calling him “an excellent player with long arms and toughness” who has returned even stronger from surgery. Meanwhile, cornerback Malcolm Hartzog is questionable this week, meaning Jamir Conn could see more playing time.

Nebraska (2-0) will host Houston Christian (1-1) at Memorial Stadium on Saturday. Kickoff is set for 11 a.m. CT.

snippet.title: Matt Rhule Shares Thoughts on Cincinnati Win, Previews Akron, WR Bonner Out with Torn ACL
snippet.description:
Nebraska head coach Matt Rhule reflected on his team’s 20-17 win over Cincinnati and looked ahead to Saturday’s home opener against Akron during his weekly press conference on Monday.

Rhule said he is eager to return to Memorial Stadium for a night game.

“Obviously, we’re excited to come back home, play at home this week versus Akron,” Rhule said. “Joe Moorhead is a coach I have a long history with and a lot of respect for.”

The Huskers will be without tight end Janiran Bonner, who suffered a season-ending torn ACL. Rhule described Bonner as a “selfless teammate” who has meant a great deal to the program.

Defensively, Nebraska gave up more than 200 rushing yards against the Bearcats, with 96 of those yards coming from quarterback runs. Rhule admitted the Huskers struggled with containment.

“Anytime you give up 200 yards rushing, you’re not gonna be real happy,” Rhule said. “I don’t know if we did a good enough job as coaches of getting our players ready.”

Still, Rhule credited his secondary for limiting Cincinnati to just 69 yards through the air.

On offense, running back Emmett Johnson carried the load, something Rhule said he wasn’t completely comfortable with.

“I wasn’t happy with it,” Rhule said. “I wanted to get Izaac and Mekhi in, but Emmett was hot. We went with Emmett.”

Rhule praised quarterback Dylan Raiola, who threw 42 passes without a turnover.

“He has more freedom than most quarterbacks in the country, and he’s right most of the time,” Rhule said. “So I thought he played excellent.”

Rhule also confirmed Raiola is healthy after appearing with ice on his hand after the Cincinnati game. “He took a hit in the game. He threw on Sunday. You’ll see him playing against Akron,” Rhule said.

Nebraska didn’t return from Kansas City until 4 a.m. Friday, altering the team’s weekly schedule. The Huskers skipped weight training that day, instead reviewing film in the afternoon. Players rested on Saturday before returning to film review and practice on Sunday.

Nebraska faces Akron on Saturday night at Memorial Stadium.

#Huskers #cornhuskers #huskerfootball #dylanraiola #danekey #emmettjohnson #nebraskafootball #nebraskafb #ncaafootball

snippet.title: Huskers Women’s Soccer Plays to 1-1 Draw Against Missouri State
snippet.description:
On a hot and windy afternoon at Barbara Hibner Field, the Nebraska women’s soccer team settled for a 1-1 draw against Missouri State in its second home match of the season.

The Huskers (1-0-1) dominated possession early, recording 20 shots in the game, but could not break through in the first half. Their best moment before the break came when goalkeeper CeCe Villa saved a Missouri State penalty, diving to her right to keep the score level.

The Bears (0-1-1) struck first in the second half, when Rowann Law scored with a left-footed strike less than 10 minutes after the restart.

Nebraska responded quickly. Ella Rudney equalized with a long-range shot that flew into the top corner, bringing the home crowd to its feet.

Both teams had chances to find a winner in the closing minutes, but Missouri State goalkeeper Ella Becker stood strong, making multiple saves to deny the Huskers.

Head coach John Walker said his team struggled with execution in the heat and wind.

“We gave up a goal and at least came back into the game and had some very good chances down the stretch,” Walker said. “At the end of the day, from a result standpoint, this is a game that we need to win.”

Nebraska travels to Oklahoma State on Aug. 21 before returning home to face Weber State on Aug. 28.

snippet.title: Political newcomer Nate Ostdiek launches campaign for NE Legislature seat
snippet.description:
With Senator Machaela Cavanaugh term-limited in 2026, political newcomer Nate Ostdiek has announced his campaign to represent central Omaha. The 22-year-old registered Democrat recently testified against the McCook Immigration Detention Facility at a hearing, criticizing the governor's approach.

"I think it's wrong and I think the governor is using it as a way to distract from his failing leadership and the rest of the Republican Party's failing leadership in the state," said Ostdiek. "They’ve decided to bully immigrants and really demonize a lot of people instead of actually addressing the problems that Nebraskans are facing."

Ostdiek emphasized the importance of transparency in his campaign. "I've always been active in legislature for the last several years since I became civically engaged. I've talked to numerous other bills and shown up to testify," he said.

Read more: https://nebraska.tv/news/local/political-newcomer-nate-ostdiek-launches-campaign-for-nebraska-senate-seat#

snippet.title: Nebraska Volleyball's Teraya Sigler Makes Immediate Impact for as True Freshman
snippet.description:
LINCOLN, Neb. — Nebraska volleyball is no stranger to reloading, whether through transfers or freshmen ready to contribute from day one. This fall, one of the brightest early storylines belongs to true freshman Teraya Sigler.

Sigler, the nation’s No. 1 overall recruit out of Horizon High School in Arizona, started Nebraska’s opening weekend matches against No. 3 Pittsburgh and No. 6 Stanford. A two-time Gatorade Arizona Player of the Year and a gold medalist with the U.S. U21 and U19 national teams, she entered Lincoln with a decorated resume and quickly found herself on the court under the sport’s brightest lights.

“The first five points I was kind of like, ‘Where am I? What am I doing?’” Sigler said. “Then my teammates reminded me we’re just playing volleyball. It’s just a big game in a big arena with a lot of people.”

Sigler, typically an outside hitter, has been adjusting to a new role to fit Nebraska’s lineup needs. She notched one kill against both Pittsburgh and Stanford, and added a service ace against the Cardinal. While her stat line was modest, her energy and composure drew praise from coaches and teammates alike.

“I love to help this team however they need me,” Sigler said. “I see it as an opportunity to prove myself and be the best I possibly can in that position.”

Nebraska has leaned on freshmen before — setter Bergen Reilly started last year en route to All-America honors, while Harper Murray and Andi Jackson each made early impacts. Sigler now joins that list, showing confidence that belies her age.

For coach Dani Busboom Kelly, Sigler’s presence on the court has already made a difference. “Her energy was so much fun,” Busboom Kelly said. “Whether she’s in the game or coming off for a rotation, she brings the same perspective and vibes.”

With her debut behind her, Sigler appears poised to grow into her role as the season continues. In front of more than 16,000 fans at Pinnacle Bank Arena, she showed the nerves didn’t last long.

She’s used to the spotlight, and Nebraska believes she’s ready to shine in it again.

snippet.title: LPD investigate murder-suicide amid domestic violence concerns.
snippet.description:
The Lincoln Police Department is investigating a murder-suicide after discovering a couple deceased inside a home. 51-year-old Jennifer McCarther, a mother of three and grandmother to five, was found with multiple gunshot wounds, while 51-year-old Sterling Jordan had a single gunshot wound to the head. Autopsies are pending to confirm the time, manner, and cause of death.

LPD Chief Michon Morrow stated that officers responded to a welfare check around 10:45 a.m. Friday, where they found McCarther and Jordan deceased. Jordan's employer had called for a welfare check the previous day when he did not report to work. Officers initially did not force entry due to the routine nature of welfare checks. However, a second call prompted further investigation, leading to the discovery.

This marks the third serious domestic violence case in Lincoln in 2025. Leah Dorge from Friendship Home noted an increase in the number of people seeking shelter and housing services, with an average of 167 people daily, a number that has tripled over the last decade. Natalie Robert-Day emphasized that domestic violence often begins with controlling behavior rather than physical violence.

McCarther's family is grieving, hoping to prevent similar tragedies. "Losing her has left a hole in our hearts that will never be filled, but her love and light continue to live on through all of us who are blessed to know her," the family said in a statement.

snippet.title: Elwood residents seek solutions after grocery store closure
snippet.description:
The Elwood Hometown Cooperative Market closed it's doors for the final time in March of this year, to the dismay of many residents. Now, the Center for Rural Affairs is investigating the factors behind the shutdown and exploring ways to ensure the availability of grocery stores in rural areas.

"It's incredibly difficult to own and operate a grocery store in a rural place, and it can also be difficult for rural grocery stores to have the purchasing power of larger competitors, such as a Dollar General or a Walmart," said Audra Espinoza, state policy manager for the Center for Rural Affairs.

Residents of Elwood, along with local grocers, a legislative aide, and other concerned parties gathered to share their experiences and discuss adaptations following the store's closure. Many now face a nearly half-hour drive to Lexington or rely on deliveries from Walmart.

To read more visit: https://nebraska.tv/news/local/elwood-residents-seek-solutions-after-grocery-store-closure#

snippet.title: Cattle prices surge to record highs at NE sale barns
snippet.description:
Two livestock markets in central Nebraska saw record-breaking feeder cattle prices last week. This a sign of long-awaited relief for ranchers and sale barn operators who’ve endured years of tight margins.

Rick Shoemaker, owner of Huss and Lexington Livestock Markets, said business is thriving as demand outpaces supply and more buyers show up ready to bid.

“It’s a great time to be running a sale barn,” said Shoemaker. “You don’t have to worry about starting them cattle and worry about someone not betting... The cattle industry —it’s a big boost for them and they’ve been needing it.”

Corey Cline, a bonded cattle dealer, echoed that sentiment. He said the recent surge is especially good news for producers across Nebraska and beyond who are finally seeing strong returns.

“It makes me feel really good for all the producers out here in Nebraska and all over the country that’s finally cashing in on some really good markets,” said Cline.

Read more: https://nebraska.tv/news/local/two-sale-barns-set-record-breaking-feeder-cattle-prices

snippet.title: Kearney police officer injured in crash while responding to emergency
snippet.description:
A Kearney Police Department officer was injured in a collision with a Buffalo County Sheriff's Office patrol vehicle while responding to a medical emergency on the morning of September 11, 2025. The accident occurred at approximately 7:00 a.m. at the intersection of North Railroad Street and K Avenue, as both vehicles were traveling east.

Following the impact, the KPD patrol vehicle struck a curb and rolled, coming to rest on its driver side. The officer was trapped inside and required extrication with the assistance of Kearney Police Department officers, Kearney Volunteer Fire Department personnel, and CHI Good Samaritan Emergency Medical Services. The officer was transported to a local hospital with non-life-threatening injuries, where he was treated and released. The BCSO deputy involved in the accident was not injured.

The Nebraska State Patrol is conducting an investigation into the accident.

Read more: https://nebraska.tv/news/local/kearney-police-officer-injured-in-crash-while-responding-to-emergency#

snippet.title: Huskers Brace for Northwestern Run Attack; Rhule Disputes Notion of Lost Focus
snippet.description:
Nebraska head coach Matt Rhule pushed back on the idea that the Huskers lost focus in preparation for last week’s loss at Minnesota, saying his team “did not take our feet off the gas pedal” as they now turn their attention to Northwestern.

The response came after tight end Luke Lindemeier said Tuesday that Nebraska lost focus in the week leading up to the 24–6 setback in Minneapolis. Rhule disagreed, emphasizing that both last week and this week have featured strong practices.

“I thought we had a great week of work,” Rhule said. “Our guys haven’t taken their foot off the gas.”

Nebraska will need that urgency against a Northwestern offense built on a physical ground game. The Wildcats are averaging 190 rushing yards per game, sixth best in the Big Ten. Rhule praised Northwestern’s offensive front and explosive running backs.

“They’re unbelievably well-coached up front,” Rhule said. “Their backs have great bursts and they’re as athletic as we’ll see. They have home-run ability.”

The matchup puts pressure on a Nebraska defense ranked 15th in the Big Ten in rushing defense, allowing 157 yards per game. However, if the Huskers can keep the Wildcats behind the sticks, the advantage flips. Nebraska owns the nation’s top-ranked pass defense, while Northwestern enters with the No. 112 passing offense.

“You can’t let 1st-and-10 become 2nd-and-5,” Rhule said. “It’s going to come down to our ability to get off blocks and make tackles.”

Nebraska received mixed injury news entering the weekend. Starting right guard Rocco Spindler, who left the Minnesota game with a finger injury, is expected to play. Rhule called the decision a matter of pain tolerance and said Spindler practiced all week. “He’s a baller,” Rhule said.

On the other side of the ledger, defensive back Malcolm Hartzog will miss the remainder of the season. He will redshirt after undergoing surgery for a core-muscle injury.

Nebraska (5–2, 2–2 Big Ten) and Northwestern (5–2, 3–1) kick off at 11 a.m. Saturday at Memorial Stadium.

snippet.title: Coach Rhule Urges Huskers to Play With ‘Killer Instinct’ Against Houston Christian
snippet.description:
Nebraska coach Matt Rhule wants his team to carry a “killer instinct” into Saturday’s game against Houston Christian.

Previewing the matchup with the FCS opponent, Rhule pointed to another Nebraskan chasing history this weekend — Omaha native Terence “Bud” Crawford. The boxer is moving up multiple weight classes to face Canelo Álvarez in a bid to become the first male unified champion in three different divisions.

“Don’t just sit there and hope that you get accolades,” Rhule said. “Risk it all. Risk it all to be great.”

Rhule said that mindset is what he wants from the Huskers all season. He emphasized playing a full 60 minutes and not letting an opponent gain life.

“You put away the people you’re supposed to put away,” Rhule said. “They’re supposed to walk up to their A.D. and be like, ‘Don’t schedule them again.’ That’s the mentality we’re supposed to have.”

Rhule reported no new injuries for Nebraska, noting his team remains healthy entering Week 3. While Houston Christian (1-1) is an FCS program, Rhule said the Huskers won’t overlook them.

“They’re from Texas. They have quality players, good schemes, great coaching,” Rhule said. “So we’re looking for the same 60 minutes of competition this week as well.”

Nebraska routed Akron 68-0 last week, its highest-scoring game since 2012. Rhule said he expects the Huskers to show the same energy against the Huskies.

snippet.title: Exclusive John Cook Interview Moments After Statue Revealed!
snippet.description:
Former Nebraska volleyball coach John Cook said it felt “a little bit eerie” to see his newly unveiled bronze statue outside the Devaney Sports Center, but he credited the moment more to the program’s legacy than to himself.

“It’s so well done. I really admire the artists that do this,” Cook said, referencing world-renowned sculptor George Lundeen. “It really doesn’t mean all that much for me. It’s more about Nebraska volleyball and all that we’ve done here.  Nebraska’s the epicenter of volleyball in this country, and I think this is just a symbol of that.”

Cook announced his retirement on Jan. 29, 2025, after 25 seasons as Nebraska's head coach. Overall, Cook spent 32 years as a college head coach - seven at Wisconsin and 25 at Nebraska. He won four NCAA championships leading the Huskers.

Cook is the first non-football coach to receive a statue. He joins legendary football coaches Tom Osborne and Bob Devaney as the third Nebraska coach immortalized with a statue. Cook said Osborne called him the day before the unveiling.

“He always downplays it so much, that’s the way he is,” Cook said. “But for me personally, he was one of my role models here. He was also the athletic director for four or five years and really helped me with the move to Devaney. To be recognized along with him means a lot.”

Cook also praised his successor, Dani Busboom Kelly, who took over the program this season.

“To me, it’s the greatest achievement of my coaching career — to be able to hire a coach from Nebraska who played here, coached here, and won national championships, and then to come back as the head coach,” Cook said. “To me, it’s my greatest accomplishment.”

Now living full-time in Wyoming, Cook said he enjoys life away from coaching but still feels a strong connection to the sport.

“I love it. I can’t tell you how much I love it,” Cook said. “I’ve been back and forth because of things like this and doing some TV matches, which I really enjoy. It helps keep me a part of it.”

Cook recently sat on the other side of the camera, interviewing Busboom Kelly for a broadcast. He said the role came naturally.

“I’m not going to be your typical color analyst or interviewer because I know Dani,” Cook said. “I feel comfortable in asking her almost anything I wanted to try to get some more out of her for the fans to understand what it’s like to coach and how she feels. It was awesome.”

Speakers at the event included University of Nebraska President Jeffrey Gold, Nebraska Director of Athletics Troy Dannen, Nebraska Volleyball Head Coach Dani Busboom Kelly, and Cook himself.

After taking over the program in 2000, Cook guided the Huskers to four national championships, 12 NCAA Semifinal appearances, nine Big 12 titles, and five Big Ten titles. He achieved a career record of 883-176 (.834), the fifth-best winning percentage all-time for a Division I volleyball coach. Cook's record of 722-103 at Nebraska was the best winning percentage (.875) for any DI program from 2000-24.

With Cook at the helm, Nebraska produced numerous individual honors, including five Olympians, a Honda-Broderick Cup winner, three AVCA Division I National Players of the Year, 72 AVCA All-Americans, three Academic All-Americans of the Year, 25 Academic All-Americans, and 10 conference players of the year. Nebraska volleyball student-athletes combined for 88 all-conference awards and 155 academic all-conference honors in Cook's program.

snippet.title: Huskers Turn to True Freshman QB TJ Lateef after Dylan Raiola Injury Ahead of UCLA Matchup
snippet.description:
Nebraska will have to find a way to avoid back-to-back losses without its starting quarterback.

Head coach Matt Rhule confirmed Monday that sophomore quarterback Dylan Raiola is out for the season with a broken fibula. Raiola, who started 22 straight games in his Nebraska career, will undergo surgery this week.

“It’s not like it’s a ‘battle your way through it’ type deal,” Rhule said. “He’s going to have a chance for once to sit there and watch and learn. I know he’ll be involved in game planning and ideas, and he’ll rally around TJ.”

Raiola finishes the season with 2,000 passing yards, 18 touchdowns, and six interceptions through nine games.

True freshman TJ Lateef will step in as Nebraska’s new starting quarterback. Lateef saw limited action in blowout wins earlier this season against Akron and Houston Christian.

“He’s really smart, always on time, knows the progressions, knows the checks — you can trust him in those things,” Rhule said. “He’s got amazing arm talent. He can spin it, he can run, he can move. He’s an excellent player, and I think everyone’s going to see him play really, really well.”

The start will be a homecoming for Lateef and five other Huskers who grew up in California. Nebraska travels to Pasadena to face UCLA under the lights at the Rose Bowl in primetime.

Rhule said UCLA, now 3-5, looks much improved after a midseason coaching change that elevated offensive coordinator Jerry Neuheisel to interim head coach.

“It’s really a tale of two seasons,” Rhule said. “You take out the first part of the year — they’re 3-2 in the Big Ten under Coach Skipper. They’ve got things rolling, so it’ll be an unbelievable battle, an unbelievable challenge.”

Another injury concern for Nebraska is offensive lineman Elijah Pritchett, who left the USC game early. Rhule said his status for UCLA will be a game-time decision, depending on pain tolerance.

snippet.title: Tar spot confirmed in western Nebraska
snippet.description:
Tar spot, a dangerous corn disease, has now been confirmed in western Nebraska. Experts warn it can reduce yields by up to 50%.

Agronomists are closely monitoring the spread of tar spot, a corn fungal disease, which is being detected in western Nebraska for the first time. Originally found in eastern states like Iowa and Minnesota, tar spot has gradually moved westward, with recent reports from Buffalo, Dawson and Custer counties.

Katelyn Duffy, CPI Precision Agronomy manager, highlighted the significance of the discovery. "We were the first to be able to identify tar spot [in the area] and also send it in for confirmation. It is a nasty disease that will can cause up to 50% yield loss if it's not caught soon enough and taken care of," she said.

To read more: https://nebraska.tv/news/local/tar-spot-disease-spreads-to-western-nebraska-raising-concerns-for-corn-yield#

snippet.title: Huskers set for first true road test at Maryland
snippet.description:
Nebraska will face its first true road game of the season this weekend as the Huskers (4-1) travel east to take on Maryland (4-1). Though they’ve already played away from Lincoln once — a win over Cincinnati in Kansas City — head coach Matt Rhule said that game felt more like a home contest. To prepare for the long trip to the East Coast, Rhule said Nebraska is adjusting its typical travel schedule.

“We’re not a big Thursday operation,” Rhule said. “We like to go on Fridays and keep our routine, but this is one time and the only time this year that we’ll go out on Thursday, just because of the nature of being on the East Coast.”

The Huskers’ defense will be tested by Maryland’s freshman quarterback Malik Washington, a former five-star recruit known for his ability to throw deep and extend plays. Washington has helped lead an offense averaging 31 points per game, with 257 yards passing and 93 yards rushing. “They can run the football, but their passing game has really opened up,” Rhule said. “We’re a pretty good pass defense, so it should be a good matchup.”

Nebraska’s offense continues to find balance behind quarterback Dylan Raiola. The Huskers are averaging 320 passing yards and 130 rushing yards per game. Both teams have excelled at protecting the football — Nebraska is plus-six in turnover margin this season, while Maryland sits at plus-seven.

“I’d rather be plus-one every game than plus-six on the year,” Rhule said. “That gives you a chance to win.”

Rhule also highlighted several defensive standouts from the Michigan State game, including Elijah Jeudy’s first career sack and Deshaun Singleton’s two interceptions in the second quarter. “It’s one thing to not give the ball away, which we’re doing a pretty good job of,” Rhule said. “But we’re also taking it away — that’s playmakers stepping up.”

On the injury front, cornerback Malcolm Hartzog remains questionable after trying to test things out pregame last week. Rhule said Hartzog would be an ideal player to have against Maryland’s high-powered passing attack, but his status will be clearer after Tuesday’s practice. 

Nebraska and Maryland kick off Saturday in College Park at 2:30 PM CST, with both teams looking to stay in the hunt near the top of the Big Ten standings.

#Huskers #cornhuskers #huskerfootball #dylanraiola #lukelindenmeyer #emmettjohnson #nebraskafootball #nebraskafb #ncaafootball

snippet.title: Nebraska Prepares for Prime-time Showdown with No. 23 USC as Rhule Signs Contract Extension
snippet.description:
The biggest news in college football on Thursday came out of Lincoln, where Nebraska head coach Matt Rhule signed a contract extension to remain with the Cornhuskers through 2032.

After finalizing the deal, Rhule quickly shifted focus to Saturday’s showdown against No. 23 USC at Memorial Stadium. The Huskers are looking to end a lengthy drought — they’re 0–28 against AP Top 25 teams since 2016.

“We’re playing a team this week coming off a bye, so you know you’re a little banged up, they’re fresh,” Rhule said. “You better be dialed in — and they’ve been dialed in all week.”

Rhule’s extension, which he called a move about “stability,” sends a clear message of commitment to fans, players, and recruits.

“I want to give this team everything I have and not be distracted with anything else,” Rhule said. “When they were able to get it done, we were able to get it done.”

Nebraska enters the weekend 6–2, its best eight-game start in nine years, but faces its toughest test yet. USC leads the Big Ten in total offense, averaging more than 530 yards and 42 points per game.

The Huskers counter with one of the league’s top pass defenses, ranking second in the conference, but will be challenged by Trojan quarterback Jayden Maiava, who has thrown for more than 2,100 yards and 19 touchdowns for the 5–2 Trojans.

On offense, Nebraska sophomore Dylan Raiola has tossed 17 touchdowns this season, while running back Emmett Johnson has surpassed 1,000 yards from scrimmage in just eight games.

Special teams could be a difference-maker. Nebraska leads the Big Ten and ranks third nationally in kickoff returns, averaging 32 yards per return. USC, meanwhile, ranks near the bottom of the nation in kick coverage, allowing 28 yards per return.

Game-time temperatures are expected to hover around 45 degrees at kickoff, with wind chills in the 30s — conditions Rhule says his team is embracing.

Kickoff is set for 6:30 p.m. Saturday, November 1.

snippet.title: Powered by the Farm and the Sun
snippet.description:
“As long as the monthly charge doesn’t get exorbitant we’ll use it as a back up. But we don’t need it. We can get by without it," says Yeager.

Last year, Yeager decided to start putting 44,000 watts of power together on his own. He created a solar panel system that energizes his home and shop.

”So it’s enough to run what I need, plus charge a couple electric vehicles, when the sun is shining."

Building and creating have always been a part of Alec Yeager's everyday life growing up on this Southwest Nebraska farm, traits that come naturally.

"My dad and I made most of our farm equipment. My uncle was a blacksmith. My grandfather was a blacksmith."

Yeager’s innovative farm ideas grew to him realizing that the sky’s the limit.

snippet.title: Nebraska Prepares for High-Stakes Matchup Against No. 23 USC
snippet.description:
Nebraska football faces a critical test Saturday night in prime time when No. 23 USC visits Memorial Stadium in a matchup that could determine the Huskers’ postseason hopes.

The 6-2 Cornhuskers enter the game with their College Football Playoff aspirations still alive, while a loss would in all likelihood end their chances. Coach Matt Rhule said the team is focused on finishing games, not just competing.

“I always want to come down the stretch of every year with a chance to make it in the College Football Playoff,” Rule said during his weekly Monday press conference. “The entire focus this week is finishing, not just competing.”

Nebraska’s recent struggles include allowing Northwestern to come back from a 15-point deficit. Rhule emphasized the importance of closing out games when holding a lead.

On defense, the Huskers face a potent 5-2 USC offense led by quarterback Jayden Maiava, who excels at both passing and running the zone read. Rule, who has coached against USC coach Lincoln Riley at Temple and Baylor before last year's matchup, called him a “brilliant offensive mind” who will find ways to score.

The Nebraska game plan will rely on discipline, physicality, and tackling in space. Offensively, the Huskers aim to lean on running back Emmitt Johnson, who ranks second in the Big Ten with 837 rushing yards and leads the conference with 1,019 all-purpose yards. Johnson is the only Big Ten player this season to surpass 1,000 all-purpose yards.

Saturday night’s game will feature the Huskers in blackout uniforms, with Rhule noting the crowd at Memorial Stadium as a distinct advantage. “It’s truly a true 12th man,” he said. “But again, it’s going to come down to blocking and tackling.”


Injury news for Nebraska includes starting right tackle Teddy Prochazka, who will miss the remainder of the season with a torn ACL. Porhaska also missed last year due to injury.

snippet.title: Huskers Football Holds Final Preseason Scrimmage
snippet.description:
Nebraska held its final preseason scrimmage on Saturday, as head coach Matt Rhule assessed where the Huskers stand two weeks ahead of their opener.

Rhule said he was pleased with the progress of his first-team units but stressed the need for more consistency from the reserves.

“I feel really good about our first teams,” Rhule said. “It’s which guys on the second team, which guys that are backups or even third team, which guys are going to step up. The quality of play dropped from the ones to the twos to the threes, and I don’t like that.”

Still, Rhule described the day as a “very successful” close to camp, noting the team is beginning to gel ahead of its Aug. 28 matchup against Cincinnati at Arrowhead Stadium.

snippet.title: Top Ranked Nebraska Sweeps Michigan to Open Big Ten Volleyball Play
snippet.description:
Top-ranked Nebraska opened Big Ten play with a sweep of Michigan on Sunday, improving to 11-0 with a 25-22, 25-10, 25-13 victory at the Bob Devaney Sports Center.

The Huskers, who entered the match unbeaten, controlled both sides of the ball after weathering an early surge from the Wolverines. Nebraska finished with 47 kills compared to Michigan’s 24, adding five service aces and 12 blocks.

Setter Bergen Reilly tallied 28 assists and moved into fifth place on Nebraska’s all-time assists list in the rally-scoring era, passing her head coach, Dani Busboom Kelly.

“Being a setter at Nebraska is such an honor, and it’s something that I’ll never forget,” Reilly said. “I hope I’m only getting started too.”

Busboom Kelly praised her team’s composure after Michigan opened with energy. “I thought we played really well. Michigan came out blazing, but we stayed composed and forced a momentum shift,” she said. “It was just an overall really good performance on both sides of the ball.”

Freshman outside hitter Virginia Adriano added offensive firepower, hitting over .400 for the match, while libero Olivia Mauch anchored the back row with steady passing and out-of-system setting.

“It was a good, complete game by her,” Busboom Kelly said of Mauch, who has started at libero after taking over the position from Laney Choboy.

The Huskers stay at home for their next match when they host Maryland Saturday at 3 p.m. CST.

snippet.title: NTV News Live Stream
Huskers Stay Undefeated, Beating the Utes 3-1 After Dropping First Set at Home This Season
snippet.description:
Courtesy of Nebraska Athletics

The No. 1 Nebraska volleyball team bounced back from an opening set loss to win 3-1 over No. 22 Utah in front of 8,632 at John Cook Arena at the Bob Devaney Sports Center on Friday night.

The Huskers dropped the first set, 21-25, but recovered strong with 25-8, 25-18, 25-13 victories to improve to 7-0 on the season. Utah fell to 5-2.

Rebekah Allick tied her career high with 13 kills on .667 hitting, along with seven blocks. Andi Jackson posted nine kills and hit .375 with five blocks. Harper Murray also recorded nine kills. Taylor Landfair notched eight kills on .467 hitting and had two aces among her team-high 27 serve attempts. Allie Sczech came off the bench to provide six kills.

Nebraska hit .302 and held Utah to .063. Bergen Reilly recorded 32 assists and 18 digs, and Laney Choboy had 12 digs.

The Huskers had 12.5 blocks to 5.0 for Utah. NU had more kills (48-34) and digs (57-44). Both teams had three aces, but Nebraska made six fewer service errors.

snippet.title: Coach DBK and All-American Setter Bergen Reilly Talk as Huskers Prepare for Utah and Grand Canyon
snippet.description:
Nebraska's All-American Setter Bergen Reilly says the Huskers’ offense is finding its rhythm as the team prepares for Utah this weekend.

“I’ve been working on consistency with hitters, and it’s been a fun offense to run,” Reilly said. “Hopefully we can keep getting better and grow throughout the season.”

Reilly credited teammates for helping elevate her play, especially freshman setter Campbell. “She’s beyond her years,” Reilly said. “I’ve even learned from her. She sets with full confidence and no fear.”

Head Coach Dani Busboom Kelly said she likes the progress her team has made, but passing remains an area for improvement.

“Our passing has been solid, but there’s always room for growth,” Busboom Kelly said. “We don’t expect perfection, but we want them to play free and know they can fail. That’s how you get better.”

Busboom Kelly emphasized the importance of enjoying wins rather than treating them with relief. “Whether it’s big or small, have fun and play free. The most important thing is what we’re doing right now,” she said.

#22 Utah, Nebraska’s next opponent, brings challenges. The Utes are known for strong serving and boast one of the nation’s top outside hitters. “Utah’s a really good serving team,” Busboom Kelly said. “We have to be ready.”

The Huskers are 6-0 entering the matchup at the Devaney Center on Friday, September 9th. Saturday, September 10th, the Huskers host Grand Canyon.

snippet.title: NTV's Athlete of the Week: Hastings St. Cecilia Volleyball Senior MB Payton Sullivan
snippet.description:
Payton Sullivan loves Volleyball. The senior from Hastings St. Cecilia knew from a young age she’d be destined for big things.

“There is a memory I have, I went to my first-ever Nebraska game and said to my mom they’re really good at volleyball, I’m probably going to be good at volleyball,” said Payton Sullivan, St. Cecila senior.

Sullivan was right. The 6’1 senior middle has a .454 hitting percentage which ranks her second in all classes and first in class C.

“She’s going to see four hands all the time, but because of her athleticism and how hard she works she knows how to swing high,” said St. Cecilia head coach Kelan Schumacher.

Sullivan’s hitting ability was on full display as the Wayne State commit garnered her 1000th career kill.

“As a middle hitter its not super easy to get 1000 kills. She works so hard and its super impressive,” said Shumacher.

“Since I was young, I’ve always wanted to be the best I can be, so I’ve put in a lot of hours in the gym plus the ability to be able to jump a little bit which helps me have better court vision,” said Sullivan.

Sullivan and her team are one win away from returning to the state playoffs.

snippet.title: Huskers Open Season Against Pitt in Showcase at PBA
snippet.description:
Nebraska volleyball opens its season in historic fashion, playing its first-ever match at Pinnacle Bank Arena on  Friday in the AVCA First Serve Showcase. The top-ranked Huskers face a stiff test against No. 3 Pittsburgh, which is coming off a 33-2 season and its fourth straight NCAA semifinal appearance.

The Panthers return AVCA National Player of the Year Olivia Babcock and a roster that coach Dan Fisher has bolstered with new additions.

Nebraska enters a new era under first-year head coach Dani Busboom Kelly, who takes over after two decades under John Cook. Busboom Kelly is no stranger to Fisher, having faced him annually while coaching at Louisville.

“I just think we made each other better,” Fisher said. “They exposed stuff that we didn’t see against some other teams, and I think we were able to do the same against them.”

The matchup is steeped in history, as Nebraska has won all 14 previous meetings against Pitt. But this time, the Panthers bring one of the nation’s toughest serving and grittiest defensive squads.

Nebraska players said the environment inside PBA feels like December volleyball.

“A lot of us were saying it felt like we’re in December already, like it really feels like a Final Four environment, which is cool,” captain junior setter Bergen Reilly said. “We know the fans are going to bring it, so that’ll make it even better.”

The Huskers won’t get much of a break. On Sunday, they’ll face No. 6 Stanford in another top-10 showdown.

“When you get to December, you’re only playing the best of the best,” Busboom Kelly said. “You have to challenge yourself. Might as well start early in the season and be prepared.”

snippet.title: Huskers Ready for Rivalry with Penn State in First Big Ten Road Test
snippet.description:
John Cook’s legendary run with Nebraska volleyball ended last season in familiar fashion — deep in December, but just short of another title.

The four-time national champion retired after the Huskers fell to Penn State in the 2024 national semifinals. The Nittany Lions went on to defeat Louisville in the final under head coach Dani Busboom Kelly, who is now in her first season leading the Nebraska program.

Busboom Kelly has the Huskers off to a 12-0 start, including 2-0 in Big Ten play, as they prepare for their first road test of the season: a trip to Penn State for the 13th match of the year.

Senior captains Rebekah Allick and Bergen Reilly aren’t hiding what the matchup means.

“I got a grudge, so I want to kick some butt,” Allick said. 

“Games like this always have implications for the postseason," Reilly said.

Allick said consistency will be key against Penn State’s All-American Kennedy Martin.

“First and second set, that kind of attack, they’re going to hit everything probably at full force,” she said. “But in the fourth and fifth set, if it goes long, you start to see tendencies and what hitters are most comfortable with.”

Reilly echoed the stakes.

“We know what our goals are, and that’s a Big Ten championship and a national championship,” Reilly said. “Our offense has been flowing, and that allows me to be more aggressive. But we know Penn State is one of the loudest gyms in the country, and we have to be ready for that.”

Busboom Kelly, who lost twice to Penn State last year with Louisville, said motivation won’t be an issue.

“I’d say we’re pretty motivated,” she said. “It’s kind of a chip-on-our-shoulder week.”

Nebraska’s trip doesn’t end in State College. The Huskers head to Rutgers the following day, but for now, all eyes are on the rivalry match.

First serve is scheduled for 7 p.m. Friday on FOX.
